METRO MATCH

3 OF 16 LIVE

Stat cards for the world’s great metro systems, every number dated and sourced. Flip a card for the lore; pick a stat and beat the cpu; one guess a day in the daily.

tokyo
the two crews

13 lines · Metro + Toei

data: OSM snapshot 2026-06-11 · ODbL · ranks: live deck of 3 · density: bbox basis
tokyo
Tokyo Metro + Toei
Thirteen lines, two operators, one grid.
Two operators share one grid: Tokyo Metro runs nine lines, Toei runs four.
G is for Ginza: Asia’s first metro line, opened 1927.
“Tokyo Subway Linemap” by Yveltal, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0
seoul
the sprawl
02/16
23456789
8 lines · 2–9 shown
3rdopened1974
1ststations607
1stspan57.7 km
3rddensity0.27 st/km²
data: OSM snapshot 2026-06-11 · ODbL · ranks: live deck of 3 · density: bbox basis
seoul
Seoul Metropolitan Subway
58 km between its furthest stations, the deck’s longest reach.
Line 2 is a 48 km loop around the city; trains circle it all day.
Many stations double as underground arcades; a few anchor whole malls.
“Seoul Metropolitan Subway network map” by Satellizer, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0
diagram dated 2023, future lines as then planned

paris
Métro de Paris
1.1 stations per square km, the deck’s tightest grid.
Opened for the 1900 World’s Fair; Line 1 still runs the route it opened with.
In central Paris you are rarely more than 500 m from a Métro entrance.
“Carte Métro de Paris” by Rigil, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0

THE DAILY ·
Which plots more stations?
vs
the stat rotates daily · the streak lives in this browser · live deck of three for now

The game

The battle. You hold a card face up; the cpu holds one face down. Pick the stat you think wins; the cards compare, and the round goes to the better number. First to three rounds takes the match.

The daily. One question a day, one guess, and a streak that lives in your browser. Nothing leaves the page.

Win directions are fixed. Opened wins earlier; stations, span, density, route-km and ridership win larger.

The six stats

statwhat it measureswins
openedearliest regular passenger service within the card’s declared scope, from operator histories, dated earlier
stationsstation complexes plotted from the frozen snapshot; interchanges counted oncemore
spanthe furthest-stations distance: the geodesic between the two stations farthest apart, computedmore
densitystations per square km of network extent; bounding-box basis today, convex-hull basis when the pipeline landsmore
route-kmreported route length, dated and sourced (almanac grade; in the pipeline)more
ridershipreported annual ridership, dated and sourced (almanac grade; in the pipeline)more

Ranks are computed across the live deck of 3; the full deck of 16 takes over at the pipeline stage. Missing evidence renders Unknown, never zero, and Unknown sits out battles.

Scope: what a card counts

Each card declares its scope as the network its city’s own familiar map draws: the rider’s network, not one operator’s books (ratified 2026-06-12; lands fully at the roster scale-up).

Today’s three cards: Seoul shows lines 2–9; the full Capital Area network, with its long shared-track corridors, is the pipeline’s hard scope question and gets its entry here when frozen. Paris is the Métro proper, lines 1–14 plus 3bis and 7bis; RER excluded. Tokyo is Tokyo Metro plus Toei, through-running truncated at the scope boundary.

The pills on every card carry a line count and a scope tag, so the card never silently claims completeness.

Sources and dates

Geometry and station counts: OpenStreetMap, via the subway preprocessor CDN, snapshot 2026-06-11, ODbL. The page is built offline from committed snapshots; raw GeoJSON never ships here.

Opened years: operator histories; per-line dated sourcing arrives with the pipeline. Route-km and annual ridership: reported figures, almanac grade; they appear when they can carry a source and an as-of date, and until then their rows read pipeline, never zero.

The diagrams on the backs

The lore side of each card carries the diagram riders actually see, as a Wikimedia Commons recreation, credited on the card:

“Seoul Metropolitan Subway network map” by Satellizer, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

“Carte Métro de Paris” by Rigil, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0

“Tokyo Subway Linemap” by Yveltal, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

Seoul’s diagram is dated 2023 and draws future lines as then planned.

Official schematic artwork appears nowhere on this site: the major operators enforce copyright on their map artwork, so the long-maintained Commons recreations are the legal route to the familiar map. Twelve of the sixteen backs are already sourced and licence-verified this way; Madrid, Copenhagen, Beijing and Guangzhou get the same audit before their cards land (their slots say so).

Why sixteen

The deck is sixteen systems, each here for a different reason, not a top-N: Tokyo (the integration story: metro, JR and private rail as one ecosystem), Seoul (the strongest daily-use megacity metro), Singapore (the controlled-system model), Hong Kong (reliability and the rail-plus-property model), Paris (the dense core), Shanghai (21st-century scale), Beijing (the capital mega-system, the longest network), London (the original), New York (the 24-hour giant), Madrid (the most network for its size), Moscow (the palace stations), Copenhagen (the small automated counter-example to biggest-equals-best), Delhi (the rapid-growth story outside China), Guangzhou (the Pearl River Delta seat), Mexico City (Latin America’s essential workhorse), Cairo (the African pioneer, 1987).

Absence is still content: Shenzhen lost the Pearl River Delta seat to Guangzhou; Osaka, São Paulo, Istanbul and Mumbai wait outside the deck.

stats © OpenStreetMap contributors · ODbL · OSM snapshot 2026-06-11 · lore diagrams credited on each card made by ajin.im
